question: 10. Given \( P(z)=z^{2}+a z+b \), a quadratic polynomial of the complex variable \( z \) with complex coefficients \( a, b \). Suppose that \( |P(z)|=1 \) for every \( z \) such that \( |z|=1 \). Prove that \( a=b=0 \).
solution:
First Solution. Let \( P(z)=z^{2}+a z+b \). Let \( a=p+i q, b=r+i s \), where \( p, q, r, s \) are real. We are given that \( |P(1)|=|P(-1)|=|P(i)| \) \( =|P(-i)|=1 \), and we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
|P(1)|^{2} & =|1+p+i q+r+i s|^{2} \\
& =1+p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}+s^{2}+2 p+2 r+2 p r+2 q s \\
|P(-1)|^{2} & =|1-p-i q+r+i s|^{2} \\
& =1+p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}+s^{2}-2 p+2 r-2 p r-2 q s \\
|P(i)|^{2} & =|-1+i p-q+r+i s|^{2} \\
& =1+p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}+s^{2}+2 q-2 r-2 q r+2 p s \\
|P(-i)|^{2} & =|-1-i p+q+r+i s|^{2} \\
& =1+p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}+s^{2}-2 q-2 r+2 q r-2 p s .
\end{aligned}
\]

Adding these equations, we get
\[
4=4+4\left(p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}+s^{2}\right)
\]

It follows that \( p=q=r=s=0, \quad \) so \( a=b=0 \).
We can equally well use a different set of roots of one. If \( \boldsymbol{\xi} \) is a primitive \( n \)th root of one, \( n>2 \), then
\[
n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left|P\left(\xi^{k}\right)\right|^{2}=n\left(1+|a|^{2}+|b|^{2}\right),
\]
and \( a=b=0 \) follows. We can also replace the sum by an integral. For all real \( \theta \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1=\left|P\left(e^{i \theta}\right)\right|^{2}= & \left(e^{2 i \theta}+a e^{i \theta}+b\right)\left(e^{-2 i \theta}+\bar{a} e^{-i \theta}+\bar{b}\right) \\
= & \bar{b} e^{2 i \theta}+(\bar{a}+a \bar{b}) e^{i \theta}+1+|a|^{2}+|b|^{2} \\
& +(a+\bar{a} \bar{b}) e^{-i \theta}+b e^{-2 i \theta} .
\end{aligned}
\]

If we integrate this over \( [0,2 \pi] \), we get
\[
2 \pi=2 \pi\left(1+|a|^{2}+|b|^{2}\right)
\]
and \( a=b=0 \), as before.
Second Solution. If \( \alpha, \beta, \gamma \) are complex numbers such that \( |\alpha|=|\beta| \) \( =|\gamma|=1 \) and \( \alpha+\beta+\gamma=3 \), then \( \alpha=\beta=\gamma=1 \). Let \( \omega= \) \( (-1+i \sqrt{3}) / 2 \), a cube root of one. Then \( |P(1)|=|\omega P(\omega)|=\left|\omega^{2} P\left(\omega^{2}\right)\right| \) \( =1 \) and \( P(1)+\omega P(\omega)+\omega^{2} P\left(\omega^{2}\right)=3 \), so
\[
P(1)=\omega P(\omega)=\omega^{2} P\left(\omega^{2}\right)=1
\]
and \( P(1)=1, P(\omega)=\omega^{2}, P\left(\omega^{2}\right)=\omega=\omega^{4} \). But there is only one polynomial of degree less than three that takes these values at these points, and it is \( P(z)=z^{2} \).

This argument also works using higher roots of one or by integration.
Third Solution. Put \( Q(z)=1+a z+b z^{2} \). Then for \( |z|=1 \), we have
\[
|Q(z)|=\left|Q(z) z^{-2}\right|=|P(z)|=1 .
\]

Since \( Q(0)=1 \), we see that \( Q \) has the same absolute value at an interior point of the disc as its maximum absolute value on the boundary. By the maximum modulus principle, \( Q \) is constant; so \( Q(z)=1 \) and \( a=b=0 \).

Remark. All three of these proofs can be adapted to prove a more general result, namely, if
\[
P(z)=z^{n}+a_{1} z^{n-1}+a_{2} z^{n-2}+\cdots+a_{n}
\]
and \( |P(z)|=1 \) for all \( z \) such that \( |z|=1 \), then
\[
a_{1}=a_{2}=\cdots=a_{n}=0
\]

Blaschke studied functions \( f \) analytic on the open unit disc and continuous on the closed disc such that \( |f(z)|=1 \) when \( |z|=1 \). He showed that all such functions have the form
\[
f(z)=\sigma \prod_{k=1}^{n} \frac{z-b_{k}}{1-\bar{b}_{k} z}
\]
where \( b_{1}, b_{2}, \ldots, b_{n} \) satisfy \( \left|b_{k}\right|<1 \) and \( \sigma \) is a constant of absolute value 1. See, for example, J. L. Walsh, Interpolation and Approximation in the Complex Domain. American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1935, pp. 281 ff.

descriptive_long.question: 10. Given \( polyfun(compvar)=compvar^{2}+coeffa\,compvar+coeffb \), a quadratic polynomial of the complex variable \( compvar \) with complex coefficients \( coeffa, coeffb \). Suppose that \( |polyfun(compvar)|=1 \) for every \( compvar \) such that \( |compvar|=1 \). Prove that \( coeffa=coeffb=0 \).
descriptive_long.solution:
First Solution. Let \( polyfun(compvar)=compvar^{2}+coeffa\,compvar+coeffb \). Let \( coeffa=realp+i\,realq,\;coeffb=realr+i\,reals \), where \( realp,realq,realr,reals \) are real. We are given that \( |polyfun(1)|=|polyfun(-1)|=|polyfun(i)|=|polyfun(-i)|=1 \), and we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
|polyfun(1)|^{2}&=|1+realp+i\,realq+realr+i\,reals|^{2}\\
&=1+realp^{2}+realq^{2}+realr^{2}+reals^{2}+2\,realp+2\,realr+2\,realp\,realr+2\,realq\,reals\\
|polyfun(-1)|^{2}&=|1-realp-i\,realq+realr+i\,reals|^{2}\\
&=1+realp^{2}+realq^{2}+realr^{2}+reals^{2}-2\,realp+2\,realr-2\,realp\,realr-2\,realq\,reals\\
|polyfun(i)|^{2}&=|-1+i\,realp-realq+realr+i\,reals|^{2}\\
&=1+realp^{2}+realq^{2}+realr^{2}+reals^{2}+2\,realq-2\,realr-2\,realq\,realr+2\,realp\,reals\\
|polyfun(-i)|^{2}&=|-1-i\,realp+realq+realr+i\,reals|^{2}\\
&=1+realp^{2}+realq^{2}+realr^{2}+reals^{2}-2\,realq-2\,realr+2\,realq\,realr-2\,realp\,reals.
\end{aligned}
\]
Adding these equations, we get
\[4=4+4\bigl(realp^{2}+realq^{2}+realr^{2}+reals^{2}\bigr)\]
It follows that \( realp=realq=realr=reals=0 \), so \( coeffa=coeffb=0 \).

We can equally well use a different set of roots of one. If \( \boldsymbol{primroot} \) is a primitive \( posintn \)th root of one, \( posintn>2 \), then
\[posintn=\sum_{counterk=1}^{posintn}\left|polyfun\bigl(primroot^{counterk}\bigr)\right|^{2}=posintn\bigl(1+|coeffa|^{2}+|coeffb|^{2}\bigr),\]
and \( coeffa=coeffb=0 \) follows. We can also replace the sum by an integral. For all real \( anglevar \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1=\left|polyfun\left(e^{i\,anglevar}\right)\right|^{2}
&=\bigl(e^{2i\,anglevar}+coeffa\,e^{i\,anglevar}+coeffb\bigr)\bigl(e^{-2i\,anglevar}+\overline{coeffa}\,e^{-i\,anglevar}+\overline{coeffb}\bigr)\\
&=\overline{coeffb}\,e^{2i\,anglevar}+\bigl(\overline{coeffa}+coeffa\,\overline{coeffb}\bigr)e^{i\,anglevar}+1+|coeffa|^{2}+|coeffb|^{2}\\
&\quad+\bigl(coeffa+\overline{coeffa}\,\overline{coeffb}\bigr)e^{-i\,anglevar}+coeffb\,e^{-2i\,anglevar}.
\end{aligned}
\]
If we integrate this over \([0,2\pi]\), we get
\[2\pi=2\pi\bigl(1+|coeffa|^{2}+|coeffb|^{2}\bigr)\]
and \( coeffa=coeffb=0 \), as before.

Second Solution. If \( alphavar,betavar,\gamma \) are complex numbers such that \(|alphavar|=|betavar|=|\gamma|=1\) and \( alphavar+betavar+\gamma=3 \), then \( alphavar=betavar=\gamma=1 \). Let \( cuberoot=(-1+i\,\sqrt{3})/2 \), a cube root of one. Then \( |polyfun(1)|=|cuberoot\,polyfun(cuberoot)|=\bigl|cuberoot^{2}\,polyfun(cuberoot^{2})\bigr|=1 \) and \( polyfun(1)+cuberoot\,polyfun(cuberoot)+cuberoot^{2}\,polyfun(cuberoot^{2})=3 \), so
\[polyfun(1)=cuberoot\,polyfun(cuberoot)=cuberoot^{2}\,polyfun(cuberoot^{2})=1\]
and \( polyfun(1)=1,\;polyfun(cuberoot)=cuberoot^{2},\;polyfun(cuberoot^{2})=cuberoot=cuberoot^{4} \). But there is only one polynomial of degree less than three that takes these values at these points, and it is \( polyfun(compvar)=compvar^{2} \).

This argument also works using higher roots of one or by integration.

Third Solution. Put \( polyaux(compvar)=1+coeffa\,compvar+coeffb\,compvar^{2} \). Then for \( |compvar|=1 \), we have
\[|polyaux(compvar)|=\left|\,polyaux(compvar)\,compvar^{-2}\right|=|polyfun(compvar)|=1.\]
Since \( polyaux(0)=1 \), we see that \( polyaux \) has the same absolute value at an interior point of the disc as its maximum absolute value on the boundary. By the maximum modulus principle, \( polyaux \) is constant; so \( polyaux(compvar)=1 \) and \( coeffa=coeffb=0 \).

Remark. All three of these proofs can be adapted to prove a more general result, namely, if
\[polyfun(compvar)=compvar^{posintn}+coeffaone\,compvar^{posintn-1}+coeffatwo\,compvar^{posintn-2}+\cdots+coeffan\]
and \( |polyfun(compvar)|=1 \) for all \( compvar \) such that \( |compvar|=1 \), then
\[coeffaone=coeffatwo=\cdots=coeffan=0\]

Blaschke studied functions \( analyticfun \) analytic on the open unit disc and continuous on the closed disc such that \( |analyticfun(compvar)|=1 \) when \( |compvar|=1 \). He showed that all such functions have the form
\[analyticfun(compvar)=unitconst\,\prod_{counterk=1}^{posintn}\frac{compvar-coeffbvar}{1-\overline{coeffbvar}\,compvar}\]
where \( coeffbvar \) satisfy \(|coeffbvar|<1\) and \( unitconst \) is a constant of absolute value 1. See, for example, J. L. Walsh, Interpolation and Approximation in the Complex Domain. American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1935, pp. 281 ff.

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 10. Given \( lanternum(quartzite)=quartzite^{2}+willowark\, quartzite+elmbranch \), a quadratic polynomial of the complex variable \( quartzite \) with complex coefficients \( willowark, elmbranch \). Suppose that \( |lanternum(quartzite)|=1 \) for every \( quartzite \) such that \( |quartzite|=1 \). Prove that \( willowark=elmbranch=0 \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
First Solution. Let \( lanternum(quartzite)=quartzite^{2}+willowark\, quartzite+elmbranch \). Let \( willowark=flintrock+i sagebrush, elmbranch=thunderer+i meadowlark \), where \( flintrock, sagebrush, thunderer, meadowlark \) are real. We are given that \( |lanternum(1)|=|lanternum(-1)|=|lanternum(i)|=|lanternum(-i)|=1 \), and we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
|lanternum(1)|^{2} & =|1+flintrock+i sagebrush+thunderer+i meadowlark|^{2} \\
& =1+flintrock^{2}+sagebrush^{2}+thunderer^{2}+meadowlark^{2}+2 flintrock+2 thunderer+2 flintrock thunderer+2 sagebrush meadowlark \\
|lanternum(-1)|^{2} & =|1-flintrock-i sagebrush+thunderer+i meadowlark|^{2} \\
& =1+flintrock^{2}+sagebrush^{2}+thunderer^{2}+meadowlark^{2}-2 flintrock+2 thunderer-2 flintrock thunderer-2 sagebrush meadowlark \\
|lanternum(i)|^{2} & =|-1+i flintrock-sagebrush+thunderer+i meadowlark|^{2} \\
& =1+flintrock^{2}+sagebrush^{2}+thunderer^{2}+meadowlark^{2}+2 sagebrush-2 thunderer-2 sagebrush thunderer+2 flintrock meadowlark \\
|lanternum(-i)|^{2} & =|-1-i flintrock+sagebrush+thunderer+i meadowlark|^{2} \\
& =1+flintrock^{2}+sagebrush^{2}+thunderer^{2}+meadowlark^{2}-2 sagebrush-2 thunderer+2 sagebrush thunderer-2 flintrock meadowlark .
\end{aligned}
\]

Adding these equations, we get
\[
4=4+4\left(flintrock^{2}+sagebrush^{2}+thunderer^{2}+meadowlark^{2}\right)
\]

It follows that \( flintrock=sagebrush=thunderer=meadowlark=0, \quad \) so \( willowark=elmbranch=0 \).

We can equally well use a different set of roots of one. If \( stargazer \) is a primitive \( riverstone \)th root of one, \( riverstone>2 \), then
\[
riverstone=\sum_{larkspore=1}^{riverstone}\left|lanternum\left(stargazer^{larkspore}\right)\right|^{2}=riverstone\left(1+|willowark|^{2}+|elmbranch|^{2}\right),
\]
and \( willowark=elmbranch=0 \) follows. We can also replace the sum by an integral. For all real \( hinterlog \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1=\left|lanternum\left(e^{i hinterlog}\right)\right|^{2}= & \left(e^{2 i hinterlog}+willowark e^{i hinterlog}+elmbranch\right)\left(e^{-2 i hinterlog}+\overline{willowark} e^{-i hinterlog}+\overline{elmbranch}\right) \\
= & \overline{elmbranch} e^{2 i hinterlog}+(\overline{willowark}+willowark \overline{elmbranch}) e^{i hinterlog}+1+|willowark|^{2}+|elmbranch|^{2} \\
& +(willowark+\overline{willowark} \overline{elmbranch}) e^{-i hinterlog}+elmbranch e^{-2 i hinterlog} .
\end{aligned}
\]

If we integrate this over \( [0,2 \pi] \), we get
\[
2 \pi=2 \pi\left(1+|willowark|^{2}+|elmbranch|^{2}\right)
\]
and \( willowark=elmbranch=0 \), as before.

Second Solution. If \( brickdust, moonwhale, \gamma \) are complex numbers such that \( |brickdust|=|moonwhale|=|\gamma|=1 \) and \( brickdust+moonwhale+\gamma=3 \), then \( brickdust=moonwhale=\gamma=1 \). Let \( cobblenet= (-1+i \sqrt{3}) / 2 \), a cube root of one. Then \( |lanternum(1)|=|cobblenet\, lanternum(cobblenet)|=\left|cobblenet^{2} lanternum\left(cobblenet^{2}\right)\right| =1 \) and \( lanternum(1)+cobblenet\, lanternum(cobblenet)+cobblenet^{2} lanternum\left(cobblenet^{2}\right)=3 \), so
\[
lanternum(1)=cobblenet\, lanternum(cobblenet)=cobblenet^{2} lanternum\left(cobblenet^{2}\right)=1
\]
and \( lanternum(1)=1, lanternum(cobblenet)=cobblenet^{2}, lanternum\left(cobblenet^{2}\right)=cobblenet=cobblenet^{4} \). But there is only one polynomial of degree less than three that takes these values at these points, and it is \( lanternum(quartzite)=quartzite^{2} \).

Third Solution. Put \( rosebloom(quartzite)=1+willowark\, quartzite+elmbranch\, quartzite^{2} \). Then for \( |quartzite|=1 \), we have
\[
|rosebloom(quartzite)|=\left|rosebloom(quartzite)\, quartzite^{-2}\right|=|lanternum(quartzite)|=1 .
\]

Since \( rosebloom(0)=1 \), we see that \( rosebloom \) has the same absolute value at an interior point of the disc as its maximum absolute value on the boundary. By the maximum modulus principle, \( rosebloom \) is constant; so \( rosebloom(quartzite)=1 \) and \( willowark=elmbranch=0 \).

Remark. All three of these proofs can be adapted to prove a more general result, namely, if
\[
lanternum(quartzite)=quartzite^{riverstone}+ambergris\, quartzite^{riverstone-1}+briarwood\, quartzite^{riverstone-2}+\cdots+ironforge
\]
and \( |lanternum(quartzite)|=1 \) for all \( quartzite \) such that \( |quartzite|=1 \), then
\[
ambergris=briarwood=\cdots=ironforge=0
\]

Blaschke studied functions \( duskflower \) analytic on the open unit disc and continuous on the closed disc such that \( |duskflower(quartzite)|=1 \) when \( |quartzite|=1 \). He showed that all such functions have the form
\[
duskflower(quartzite)=gossamer \prod_{larkspore=1}^{riverstone} \frac{quartzite-maplekey}{1-\overline{maplekey}\, quartzite}
\]
where \( b_{1}, b_{2}, \ldots, b_{riverstone} \) satisfy \( \left|b_{k}\right|<1 \) and \( gossamer \) is a constant of absolute value 1. See, for example, J. L. Walsh, Interpolation and Approximation in the Complex Domain. American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1935, pp. 281 ff.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 10. Given \( noisefunc(realaxis)=realaxis^{2}+emptiness realaxis+zerohood \), a quadratic polynomial of the complex variable \( realaxis \) with complex coefficients \( emptiness, zerohood \). Suppose that \( |noisefunc(realaxis)|=1 \) for every \( realaxis \) such that \( |realaxis|=1 \). Prove that \( emptiness=zerohood=0 \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
First Solution. Let \( noisefunc(realaxis)=realaxis^{2}+emptiness realaxis+zerohood \). Let \( emptiness=stillness+i calmness, zerohood=reststate+i quietude \), where \( stillness, calmness, reststate, quietude \) are real. We are given that \( |noisefunc(1)|=|noisefunc(-1)|=|noisefunc(i)|=|noisefunc(-i)|=1 \), and we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
|noisefunc(1)|^{2} & =|1+stillness+i calmness+reststate+i quietude|^{2} \\
& =1+stillness^{2}+calmness^{2}+reststate^{2}+quietude^{2}+2 stillness+2 reststate+2 stillness reststate+2 calmness quietude \\
|noisefunc(-1)|^{2} & =|1-stillness-i calmness+reststate+i quietude|^{2} \\
& =1+stillness^{2}+calmness^{2}+reststate^{2}+quietude^{2}-2 stillness+2 reststate-2 stillness reststate-2 calmness quietude \\
|noisefunc(i)|^{2} & =|-1+i stillness-calmness+reststate+i quietude|^{2} \\
& =1+stillness^{2}+calmness^{2}+reststate^{2}+quietude^{2}+2 calmness-2 reststate-2 calmness reststate+2 stillness quietude \\
|noisefunc(-i)|^{2} & =|-1-i stillness+calmness+reststate+i quietude|^{2} \\
& =1+stillness^{2}+calmness^{2}+reststate^{2}+quietude^{2}-2 calmness-2 reststate+2 calmness reststate-2 stillness quietude .
\end{aligned}
\]

Adding these equations, we get
\[
4=4+4\left(stillness^{2}+calmness^{2}+reststate^{2}+quietude^{2}\right)
\]

It follows that \( stillness=calmness=reststate=quietude=0, \quad \) so \( emptiness=zerohood=0 \).

We can equally well use a different set of roots of one. If \( stationary \) is a primitive \( infinite \)th root of one, \( infinite>2 \), then
\[
infinite=\sum_{continuum=1}^{infinite}\left|noisefunc\left(stationary^{continuum}\right)\right|^{2}=infinite\left(1+|emptiness|^{2}+|zerohood|^{2}\right),
\]
and \( emptiness=zerohood=0 \) follows. We can also replace the sum by an integral. For all real \( flatplane \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1=\left|noisefunc\left(e^{i flatplane}\right)\right|^{2}= & \left(e^{2 i flatplane}+emptiness e^{i flatplane}+zerohood\right)\left(e^{-2 i flatplane}+\overline{emptiness} e^{-i flatplane}+\overline{zerohood}\right) \\
= & \overline{zerohood} e^{2 i flatplane}+(\overline{emptiness}+emptiness \overline{zerohood}) e^{i flatplane}+1+|emptiness|^{2}+|zerohood|^{2} \\
& +(emptiness+\overline{emptiness} \overline{zerohood}) e^{-i flatplane}+zerohood e^{-2 i flatplane} .
\end{aligned}
\]

If we integrate this over \( [0,2 \pi] \), we get
\[
2 \pi=2 \pi\left(1+|emptiness|^{2}+|zerohood|^{2}\right)
\]
and \( emptiness=zerohood=0 \), as before.

Second Solution. If \( terminal, finality, \gamma \) are complex numbers such that \( |terminal|=|finality|=|\gamma|=1 \) and \( terminal+finality+\gamma=3 \), then \( terminal=finality=\gamma=1 \). Let \( inertness= (-1+i \sqrt{3}) / 2 \), a cube root of one. Then \( |noisefunc(1)|=|inertness noisefunc(inertness)|=\left|inertness^{2} noisefunc\left(inertness^{2}\right)\right|=1 \) and \( noisefunc(1)+inertness noisefunc(inertness)+inertness^{2} noisefunc\left(inertness^{2}\right)=3 \), so
\[
noisefunc(1)=inertness noisefunc(inertness)=inertness^{2} noisefunc\left(inertness^{2}\right)=1
\]

and \( noisefunc(1)=1, noisefunc(inertness)=inertness^{2}, noisefunc\left(inertness^{2}\right)=inertness=inertness^{4} \). But there is only one polynomial of degree less than three that takes these values at these points, and it is \( noisefunc(realaxis)=realaxis^{2} \).

This argument also works using higher roots of one or by integration.

Third Solution. Put \( constantval(realaxis)=1+emptiness realaxis+zerohood realaxis^{2} \). Then for \( |realaxis|=1 \), we have
\[
|constantval(realaxis)|=\left|constantval(realaxis) realaxis^{-2}\right|=|noisefunc(realaxis)|=1 .
\]

Since \( constantval(0)=1 \), we see that \( constantval \) has the same absolute value at an interior point of the disc as its maximum absolute value on the boundary. By the maximum modulus principle, \( constantval \) is constant; so \( constantval(realaxis)=1 \) and \( emptiness=zerohood=0 \).

Remark. All three of these proofs can be adapted to prove a more general result, namely, if
\[
noisefunc(realaxis)=realaxis^{infinite}+fullnessone realaxis^{infinite-1}+fullnesstwo realaxis^{infinite-2}+\cdots+fullnessmax
\]

and \( |noisefunc(realaxis)|=1 \) for all \( realaxis \) such that \( |realaxis|=1 \), then
\[
fullnessone=fullnesstwo=\cdots=fullnessmax=0
\]

Blaschke studied functions \( misfunction \) analytic on the open unit disc and continuous on the closed disc such that \( |misfunction(realaxis)|=1 \) when \( |realaxis|=1 \). He showed that all such functions have the form
\[
misfunction(realaxis)=variability \prod_{continuum=1}^{infinite} \frac{realaxis-emptymark}{1-\overline{emptymark} realaxis}
\]
where \( emptymark \) satisfy \( \left|emptymark\right|<1 \) and \( variability \) is a constant of absolute value 1. See, for example, J. L. Walsh, Interpolation and Approximation in the Complex Domain. American Mathematical Society, Providence, R.I., 1935, pp. 281 ff.

garbled_string.question:
<<<
10. Given \( hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{2}+prhxlvsq qzxwvtnp+kmqdwnrz \), a quadratic polynomial of the complex variable \( qzxwvtnp \) with complex coefficients \( prhxlvsq, kmqdwnrz \). Suppose that \( |hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)|=1 \) for every \( qzxwvtnp \) such that \( |qzxwvtnp|=1 \). Prove that \( prhxlvsq=kmqdwnrz=0 \).
>>>
garbled_string.solution:
<<<
First Solution. Let \( hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{2}+prhxlvsq qzxwvtnp+kmqdwnrz \). Let \( prhxlvsq=lsvqdrxm+i xkprznmh, kmqdwnrz=cdjhspqv+i fzktmnlw \), where \( lsvqdrxm, xkprznmh, cdjhspqv, fzktmnlw \) are real. We are given that \( |hjgrksla(1)|=|hjgrksla(-1)|=|hjgrksla(i)| =|hjgrksla(-i)|=1 \), and we find
\[
\begin{aligned}
|hjgrksla(1)|^{2} & =|1+lsvqdrxm+i xkprznmh+cdjhspqv+i fzktmnlw|^{2} \\
& =1+lsvqdrxm^{2}+xkprznmh^{2}+cdjhspqv^{2}+fzktmnlw^{2}+2 lsvqdrxm+2 cdjhspqv+2 lsvqdrxm\,cdjhspqv+2 xkprznmh\,fzktmnlw \\
|hjgrksla(-1)|^{2} & =|1-lsvqdrxm-i xkprznmh+cdjhspqv+i fzktmnlw|^{2} \\
& =1+lsvqdrxm^{2}+xkprznmh^{2}+cdjhspqv^{2}+fzktmnlw^{2}-2 lsvqdrxm+2 cdjhspqv-2 lsvqdrxm\,cdjhspqv-2 xkprznmh\,fzktmnlw \\
|hjgrksla(i)|^{2} & =|-1+i lsvqdrxm-xkprznmh+cdjhspqv+i fzktmnlw|^{2} \\
& =1+lsvqdrxm^{2}+xkprznmh^{2}+cdjhspqv^{2}+fzktmnlw^{2}+2 xkprznmh-2 cdjhspqv-2 xkprznmh\,cdjhspqv+2 lsvqdrxm\,fzktmnlw \\
|hjgrksla(-i)|^{2} & =|-1-i lsvqdrxm+xkprznmh+cdjhspqv+i fzktmnlw|^{2} \\
& =1+lsvqdrxm^{2}+xkprznmh^{2}+cdjhspqv^{2}+fzktmnlw^{2}-2 xkprznmh-2 cdjhspqv+2 xkprznmh\,cdjhspqv-2 lsvqdrxm\,fzktmnlw .
\end{aligned}
\]

Adding these equations, we get
\[
4=4+4\left(lsvqdrxm^{2}+xkprznmh^{2}+cdjhspqv^{2}+fzktmnlw^{2}\right)
\]

It follows that \( lsvqdrxm=xkprznmh=cdjhspqv=fzktmnlw=0 \), so \( prhxlvsq=kmqdwnrz=0 \).

We can equally well use a different set of roots of one. If \( vlmkrgsa \) is a primitive \( qfrvzngc \)th root of one, \( qfrvzngc>2 \), then
\[
qfrvzngc=\sum_{nmbtczal=1}^{qfrvzngc}\left|hjgrksla\left(vlmkrgsa^{nmbtczal}\right)\right|^{2}=qfrvzngc\left(1+|prhxlvsq|^{2}+|kmqdwnrz|^{2}\right),
\]
and \( prhxlvsq=kmqdwnrz=0 \) follows. We can also replace the sum by an integral. For all real \( jhncptoa \), we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
1=\left|hjgrksla\left(e^{i jhncptoa}\right)\right|^{2}= & \left(e^{2 i jhncptoa}+prhxlvsq e^{i jhncptoa}+kmqdwnrz\right)\left(e^{-2 i jhncptoa}+\overline{prhxlvsq} e^{-i jhncptoa}+\overline{kmqdwnrz}\right) \\
= & \overline{kmqdwnrz} e^{2 i jhncptoa}+(\overline{prhxlvsq}+prhxlvsq \overline{kmqdwnrz}) e^{i jhncptoa}+1+|prhxlvsq|^{2}+|kmqdwnrz|^{2} \\
& +(prhxlvsq+\overline{prhxlvsq}\,\overline{kmqdwnrz}) e^{-i jhncptoa}+kmqdwnrz e^{-2 i jhncptoa} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Integrating over \( [0,2\pi] \) gives
\[
2\pi=2\pi\left(1+|prhxlvsq|^{2}+|kmqdwnrz|^{2}\right),
\]
so \( prhxlvsq=kmqdwnrz=0 \), as before.

Second Solution. If \( wqdmslke, rtzgnpse, \gamma \) are complex numbers such that \( |wqdmslke|=|rtzgnpse|=|\gamma|=1 \) and \( wqdmslke+rtzgnpse+\gamma=3 \), then \( wqdmslke=rtzgnpse=\gamma=1 \). Let \( sdlqwmna=(-1+i\sqrt{3})/2 \), a cube root of one. Then \( |hjgrksla(1)|=|sdlqwmna hjgrksla(sdlqwmna)|=\left|sdlqwmna^{2} hjgrksla\left(sdlqwmna^{2}\right)\right|=1 \) and \( hjgrksla(1)+sdlqwmna hjgrksla(sdlqwmna)+sdlqwmna^{2} hjgrksla\left(sdlqwmna^{2}\right)=3 \), so
\[
hjgrksla(1)=sdlqwmna hjgrksla(sdlqwmna)=sdlqwmna^{2} hjgrksla\left(sdlqwmna^{2}\right)=1.
\]
Hence \( hjgrksla(1)=1, \; hjgrksla(sdlqwmna)=sdlqwmna^{2}, \; hjgrksla\left(sdlqwmna^{2}\right)=sdlqwmna=sdlqwmna^{4} \). There is only one polynomial of degree less than three taking these values at these points, namely \( hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{2} \).

This argument also works using higher roots of one or by integration.

Third Solution. Put \( gpkzlqra(qzxwvtnp)=1+prhxlvsq qzxwvtnp+kmqdwnrz qzxwvtnp^{2} \). For \( |qzxwvtnp|=1 \),
\[
|gpkzlqra(qzxwvtnp)|=\left|gpkzlqra(qzxwvtnp)qzxwvtnp^{-2}\right|=|hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)|=1.
\]
Because \( gpkzlqra(0)=1 \), the maximum modulus principle forces \( gpkzlqra \) to be constant; thus \( gpkzlqra(qzxwvtnp)=1 \) and \( prhxlvsq=kmqdwnrz=0 \).

Remark. The proofs extend: if
\[
hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{qfrvzngc}+gcsvrnpe qzxwvtnp^{qfrvzngc-1}+vrqmsdth qzxwvtnp^{qfrvzngc-2}+\cdots+frqdlkzp
\]
and \( |hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp)|=1 \) for all \( |qzxwvtnp|=1 \), then
\[
gcsvrnpe=vrqmsdth=\cdots=frqdlkzp=0.
\]

Blaschke studied functions \( zxqmplra \) analytic on the open unit disc and continuous on the closed disc with \( |zxqmplra(qzxwvtnp)|=1 \) when \( |qzxwvtnp|=1 \). He showed
\[
zxqmplra(qzxwvtnp)=bhzmtwqa\prod_{nmbtczal=1}^{qfrvzngc}\frac{qzxwvtnp-hnszqvle_{nmbtczal}}{1-\overline{hnszqvle_{nmbtczal}}\,qzxwvtnp},
\]
where \( hnszqvle_{1},hnszqvle_{2},\ldots,hnszqvle_{qfrvzngc} \) satisfy \( |hnszqvle_{nmbtczal}|<1 \) and \( bhzmtwqa \) has absolute value 1. (See J. L. Walsh, Interpolation and Approximation in the Complex Domain, AMS, Providence, 1935, pp. 281 ff.)
>>>

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ and $n\ge 3$ be fixed integers.  
For a multi-index $\alpha=(\alpha_{1},\ldots,\alpha_{m})\in\mathbb Z_{\ge 0}^{\,m}$ put  
$|\alpha|=\alpha_{1}+\cdots+\alpha_{m}$ and $z^{\alpha}=z_{1}^{\alpha_{1}}\cdots z_{m}^{\alpha_{m}}$.

Consider a non-zero polynomial in $m$ variables  
\[
P(z_{1},\ldots,z_{m})=\sum_{|\alpha|\le n} a_{\alpha}\,z^{\alpha},\qquad a_{\alpha}\in\mathbb C.
\]

Assume that on the $m$-dimensional unit torus  
\[
\mathbb T^{m}= \bigl\{(z_{1},\ldots,z_{m})\in\mathbb C^{m}\; :\; |z_{1}|=\cdots=|z_{m}|=1 \bigr\}
\]
the modulus of $P$ is constant: there exists $K>0$ such that  
\[
|P(z_{1},\ldots,z_{m})|\equiv K\qquad\text{for every }(z_{1},\ldots,z_{m})\in\mathbb T^{m}. \tag{$\star$}
\]

(a) Prove that there is exactly one multi-index $\alpha^{0}$ with $|\alpha^{0}|\le n$ for which $a_{\alpha^{0}}\neq 0$.

(b) Show that necessarily $|a_{\alpha^{0}}|=K$ and that, after multiplying $P$ by a complex number of modulus $1$ and possibly permuting the variables, one obtains  
\[
P(z_{1},\ldots,z_{m})=K\cdot z_{1}^{\alpha^{0}_{1}}\,z_{2}^{\alpha^{0}_{2}}\cdots z_{m}^{\alpha^{0}_{m}}.
\]

(c) Deduce that if $\deg P=n$ then $|\alpha^{0}|=n$; hence, among all degree-$n$ polynomials in $m$ variables, the only ones whose modulus is constant on $\mathbb T^{m}$ are unimodular rotations of a single monomial.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 - Fourier expansion on the torus  
Write $z_{j}=e^{i\theta_{j}}$ with $\theta_{j}\in[0,2\pi)$, and endow $\mathbb T^{m}$ with the normalized Haar measure  
\[
d\mu(\theta)=\frac{d\theta_{1}\cdots d\theta_{m}}{(2\pi)^{m}}.
\]
The characters $e^{i\langle\gamma,\theta\rangle}$, $\gamma\in\mathbb Z^{m}$, form an orthonormal basis of $L^{2}(\mathbb T^{m})$.  
For the finite support  
\[
S=\{\alpha\in\mathbb Z_{\ge 0}^{\,m}\; :\; a_{\alpha}\ne 0\}
\]
we have  
\[
P(e^{i\theta})=\sum_{\alpha\in S} a_{\alpha}\,e^{i\langle\alpha,\theta\rangle},
\]
hence  
\[
|P(e^{i\theta})|^{2}=\sum_{\alpha,\beta\in S} a_{\alpha}\,\overline{a_{\beta}}\,
e^{i\langle\alpha-\beta,\theta\rangle}. \tag{1}
\]

Step 2 - The correlation identities  
By hypothesis $(\star)$ the left-hand side of (1) equals the constant $K^{2}$, whose Fourier series has only the zero frequency.  Identifying the Fourier coefficients we obtain  
\[
\sum_{\alpha\in\mathbb Z^{m}} a_{\alpha+\gamma}\,\overline{a_{\alpha}}=0\quad(\gamma\neq 0),\tag{2}
\]
\[
\sum_{\alpha\in S}|a_{\alpha}|^{2}=K^{2}.\tag{3}
\]
(The sums in (2)-(3) are taken over all $\alpha\in\mathbb Z^{m}$, with $a_{\alpha}=0$ when $\alpha\notin S$.)

Step 3 - Extreme indices isolate a single summand  
Assume, for a contradiction, that $S$ contains at least two distinct indices.  Equip $\mathbb Z^{m}$ with the lexicographic order and set  
\[
\alpha^{-}=\min\nolimits_{\text{lex}}S,\qquad
\alpha^{+}=\max\nolimits_{\text{lex}}S,\qquad
\gamma=\alpha^{+}-\alpha^{-}\ne 0.
\]

Claim.  If $\alpha\in S$ and $\alpha+\gamma\in S$, then necessarily $\alpha=\alpha^{-}$ and $\alpha+\gamma=\alpha^{+}$; consequently the sum in (2) corresponding to this particular $\gamma$ reduces to the single term $a_{\alpha^{+}}\,\overline{a_{\alpha^{-}}}$.

Proof.  
Suppose $\alpha\in S$ with $\alpha+\gamma\in S$.  Because $\alpha^{-}$ is lexicographically minimal, $\alpha\ge\alpha^{-}$.  Let $k$ be the first coordinate where $\alpha$ and $\alpha^{-}$ differ, so $\alpha_{k}>(\alpha^{-})_{k}$.  For $j<k$ we have $\alpha_{j}=(\alpha^{-})_{j}$, hence  
\[
(\alpha+\gamma)_{j}=\alpha_{j}+\gamma_{j}
=(\alpha^{-})_{j}+(\alpha^{+}-\alpha^{-})_{j}=(\alpha^{+})_{j}.
\]
In coordinate $k$ we compute  
\[
(\alpha+\gamma)_{k}
=\alpha_{k}+\gamma_{k}
>(\alpha^{-})_{k}+\gamma_{k}
=(\alpha^{-})_{k}+(\alpha^{+})_{k}-(\alpha^{-})_{k}
=(\alpha^{+})_{k}.
\]
Hence $\alpha+\gamma$ is lexicographically larger than $\alpha^{+}$ unless $\alpha_{k}=(\alpha^{-})_{k}$, contradicting the choice of $k$.  Therefore $k$ cannot exist, i.e.\ $\alpha=\alpha^{-}$ and $\alpha+\gamma=\alpha^{+}$, proving the claim.

Accordingly  
\[
\sum_{\alpha}a_{\alpha+\gamma}\,\overline{a_{\alpha}}
=a_{\alpha^{+}}\,\overline{a_{\alpha^{-}}}. \tag{4}
\]

Step 4 - Reaching the contradiction  
Equation (2) with this $\gamma$ forces the left-hand side of (4) to vanish, yet both $a_{\alpha^{+}}$ and $a_{\alpha^{-}}$ are non-zero by definition of $S$.  Thus $a_{\alpha^{+}}\,\overline{a_{\alpha^{-}}}=0$, a contradiction.  Therefore $S$ cannot contain two different indices; it consists of exactly one multi-index, which we denote $\alpha^{0}$.  This proves part (a).

Step 5 - The size of the lone coefficient  
With only one non-zero coefficient, (3) yields $|a_{\alpha^{0}}|^{2}=K^{2}$, hence $|a_{\alpha^{0}}|=K$.  Multiplying $P$ by a unimodular constant we may assume $a_{\alpha^{0}}=K$, and obtain  
\[
P(z_{1},\ldots,z_{m})=K\cdot z^{\alpha^{0}}
=K\cdot z_{1}^{\alpha^{0}_{1}}\cdots z_{m}^{\alpha^{0}_{m}}.
\]
Permuting the coordinates if desired gives the form stated in (b).

Step 6 - The degree condition  
If $\deg P=n$, then necessarily $|\alpha^{0}|=n$; otherwise $\deg P<|\alpha^{0}|$ or $|\alpha^{0}|<n$, contradicting the definition of degree.  Hence the only degree-$n$ polynomials with constant modulus on $\mathbb T^{m}$ are unimodular rotations of the monomial $z^{\alpha^{0}}$, establishing part (c).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher‐dimensional setting:  The problem moves from one complex variable to m ≥ 2 variables; the domain is the m-torus rather than the unit circle.  
2. Additional structures:  Multi-index notation, Fourier analysis on 𝕋^{m}, orthogonality of exponentials, and combinatorial arguments about support sets are required.  
3. Deeper theory:  The solution uses the full Fourier expansion of |P|² and exploits its Toeplitz-type structure; understanding Haar measure and orthogonality on compact abelian groups is essential.  
4. Non-trivial uniqueness step:  One must choose a frequency γ that yields a single contribution in the convolution sum (2)—a subtle combinatorial observation absent from the one-variable case.  
5. More steps:  The proof must pass through Fourier coefficients, orthogonality, support analysis, contradiction, and normalization, far richer than the single-variable counterpart.

Hence the variant is substantially more technical and conceptually demanding than both the original quadratic problem and the current degree-n one-variable kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let m \geq  2 and n \geq  3 be fixed integers.  
For a multi-index \alpha  = (\alpha _1,\ldots ,\alpha _m) \in  \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}^{\,m} put |\alpha |=\alpha _1+\cdots +\alpha _m and  
z^\alpha  = z_1^{\alpha _1}\cdots z_m^{\alpha _m}.  

Consider a non-zero polynomial in m variables

  P(z_1,\ldots ,z_m)= \sum _{|\alpha |\leq  n} a_\alpha  z^\alpha  ,  a_\alpha \in \mathbb{C}.

Assume that on the m-dimensional unit torus  

  T^{m} = { (z_1,\ldots ,z_m) \in  \mathbb{C}^{m} : |z_1|=\cdots =|z_m|=1 }  

the modulus of P is constant: there exists K>0 such that  

  |P(z_1,\ldots ,z_m)| \equiv  K  for every (z_1,\ldots ,z_m) \in  T^{m}.        (\star )

(a) Prove that there is exactly one multi-index \alpha ^0 with |\alpha ^0|\leq n for which a_{\alpha ^0}\neq 0.  

(b) Show that necessarily |a_{\alpha ^0}|=K and that, after multiplying P by a complex number of modulus 1 and possibly permuting the variables, one has  

  P(z_1,\ldots ,z_m)=K\cdot z_1^{\alpha ^0_1}z_2^{\alpha ^0_2}\cdots z_m^{\alpha ^0_m}.  

(c) Deduce that if deg P=n then |\alpha ^0|=n; hence, among all degree-n polynomials in m variables, the only ones whose modulus is constant on T^{m} are unimodular rotations of a single monomial.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 - Fourier expansion on the torus  
Write z_j=e^{i\theta _j}, \theta _j\in [0,2\pi ).  With the normalized Haar measure  
d\mu (\theta )= (2\pi )^{-m}d\theta _1\cdots d\theta _m, the characters e^{i\langle \gamma ,\theta \rangle }(\gamma \in \mathbb{Z}^{m}) form an
orthonormal basis of L^2(T^{m}).  For the finite set  
S = {\alpha  : a_\alpha  \neq  0} we have  

  P(e^{i\theta }) = \Sigma _{\alpha \in S} a_\alpha  e^{i\langle \alpha ,\theta \rangle },  

so

  |P(e^{i\theta })|^2 = \Sigma _{\alpha ,\beta \in S} a_\alpha   \overline{a_\beta }\; e^{i\langle \alpha -\beta ,\theta \rangle }.        (1)

Step 2 - The correlation identities  
By (\star ) the left side of (1) equals the constant K^2, whose Fourier series
contains only the zero frequency.  Therefore, for every \gamma \neq 0,

  \Sigma _{\alpha \in \mathbb{Z}^{m}} a_{\alpha +\gamma }\,\overline{a_{\alpha }} = 0,                                 (2)

while for \gamma =0 we get  

  \Sigma _{\alpha \in S}|a_{\alpha }|^2 = K^2.                                                        (3)

(The sums in (2)-(3) may be taken over all \alpha , extending a_\alpha  by 0 outside S.)

Step 3 - Extreme indices isolate a single summand  
Assume, for a contradiction, that S contains at least two distinct indices.
Equip \mathbb{Z}^{m} with the lexicographic order.  Let  

  \alpha ^- = lexicographically minimal element of S,  
  \alpha ^+ = lexicographically maximal element of S,

and set \gamma  = \alpha ^+ - \alpha ^-, so \gamma  \neq  0.

Claim.  If \alpha \in S and \alpha +\gamma \in S, then \alpha =\alpha ^- and \alpha +\gamma =\alpha ^+; in particular,
for this \gamma  the sum in (2) reduces to the single term
a_{\alpha ^+}\,\overline{a_{\alpha ^-}}.

Proof.  
Suppose \alpha \in S with \alpha +\gamma \in S.  
Because \alpha ^- is lexicographically minimal, \alpha  \geq  \alpha ^- (lex).  
Write k for the first coordinate in which \alpha  differs from \alpha ^-.
Then \alpha _k > (\alpha ^-)_k.  For j<k we have \alpha _j = (\alpha ^-)_j, hence
(\alpha +\gamma )_j = \alpha _j + \gamma _j = (\alpha ^-)_j + (\alpha ^+-\alpha ^-)_j = (\alpha ^+)_j.  
In coordinate k we get  

  (\alpha +\gamma )_k = \alpha _k + \gamma _k  
             > (\alpha ^-)_k + \gamma _k = (\alpha ^-)_k + (\alpha ^+)_k - (\alpha ^-)_k = (\alpha ^+)_k.

Thus \alpha +\gamma  exceeds \alpha ^+ in the first coordinate where the two differ,
so \alpha +\gamma  is lexicographically larger than \alpha ^+.  
But \alpha ^+ was chosen lex-maximal in S, so \alpha +\gamma \notin S unless \alpha +\gamma =\alpha ^+.
Consequently \alpha _k = (\alpha ^-)_k, contradicting \alpha _k>(\alpha ^-)_k unless k does not exist,
i.e. \alpha =\alpha ^-.  Then \alpha +\gamma =\alpha ^-+\gamma =\alpha ^+, proving the claim.

Therefore

  \Sigma _{\alpha } a_{\alpha +\gamma }\,\overline{a_{\alpha }} = a_{\alpha ^+}\,\overline{a_{\alpha ^-}}.                (4)

Step 4 - Reaching the contradiction  
By (2) the left side of (4) must be zero, yet both coefficients
a_{\alpha ^+} and a_{\alpha ^-} are non-zero by definition of S.
Hence a_{\alpha ^+}\,\overline{a_{\alpha ^-}} = 0, a contradiction.
Thus S cannot contain two different indices; it consists of a single
multi-index, which we denote \alpha ^0.  This establishes (a).

Step 5 - The size of the lone coefficient  
With only one non-zero coefficient, (3) gives |a_{\alpha ^0}|^2 = K^2,
so |a_{\alpha ^0}|=K.  Multiplying P by a unimodular constant we may suppose
a_{\alpha ^0}=K, obtaining  

  P(z_1,\ldots ,z_m)=K\cdot z^{\alpha ^0}.  

Permuting the coordinates if desired yields the form stated in (b).

Step 6 - The degree condition  
If deg P=n, then necessarily |\alpha ^0|=n; otherwise deg P<|\alpha ^0| or |\alpha ^0|<n,
contradicting the definition of degree.  Hence the only degree-n
polynomials with constant modulus on T^{m} are unimodular rotations
of the monomial z^{\alpha ^0}=z_1^{\alpha ^0_1}\cdots z_m^{\alpha ^0_m}, completing (c).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher‐dimensional setting:  The problem moves from one complex variable to m ≥ 2 variables; the domain is the m-torus rather than the unit circle.  
2. Additional structures:  Multi-index notation, Fourier analysis on 𝕋^{m}, orthogonality of exponentials, and combinatorial arguments about support sets are required.  
3. Deeper theory:  The solution uses the full Fourier expansion of |P|² and exploits its Toeplitz-type structure; understanding Haar measure and orthogonality on compact abelian groups is essential.  
4. Non-trivial uniqueness step:  One must choose a frequency γ that yields a single contribution in the convolution sum (2)—a subtle combinatorial observation absent from the one-variable case.  
5. More steps:  The proof must pass through Fourier coefficients, orthogonality, support analysis, contradiction, and normalization, far richer than the single-variable counterpart.

Hence the variant is substantially more technical and conceptually demanding than both the original quadratic problem and the current degree-n one-variable kernel variant.
